channel.title: RPG Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in RPG Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Would You Play A Game That...?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to ask you a question: Would you play a game?

That? What I mean by this is I’m going to describe several game ideas and I’m curious if you would play that game. I thought this would be a fun Friday to do, because each one I’m going to talk about a very specific feature and what it may mean about the game. And, by the way, in g, by game I mean role playing game. Well, I mean role playing video game. I have a whole video called what’s an, what is an RPG? That’s what I’m talking about here, Not what you call an RPG, what I call an RPG. And then consider that RPG with one of these features. By the way, some of these may sound like: hey, I played a game like that in this other genre. Great, I’m talking about when you play a character in an RPG and the play varies based on that character.

item.title: Mini Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about mini games.

This comes from a very long set of questions from Mr Doomka. So let me read his questions first, then I want to talk about mini games. Hey, Tim, I understand your mistrust in miniames, but don’t they make the games more immersive? I feel like it does, for me, though the miniames are usually a gross o oversimplification of crafts, they let you feel like you’re actually doing something — lockpicking, hackpicking, etc.

item.title: Restricting Content Based On Player Choice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about restricting content based on player choice.

What do I mean by that? I’m talking about if you’ve ever played a game where there’s some content that you cannot see, based on something that you chose to do with your player. I’m not talking about restrictions on an options menu. I’m not talking about like you decided to play the lowest difficulty mode and because of that, you won’t see a certain hard boss monster. Or, when you do, they don’t use certain attacks with certain special effects, because that doesn’t occur unless you’re playing it on normal or hard difficulty.

item.title: Try Roleplaying
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about trying role playing.

You may be wondering what I’m saying, because you’re probably already thinking you’re doing that You’re playing role playing games. You’re playing a lot of RPGs and you’re playing games that say they have RPG elements. But I think in a lot of those people don’t really do role playing. At best they may be handed a role and go: “Okay, that’s what I’m going to play”. But let me walk through what I mean.

item.title: Escort Quests
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about escort quests.

A while back I did a video called RPG features. Nobody asked for and I included escort quests on that video. Why? I’ve never seen a review asking for more escort quests ever. Not once I’ve seen reviews going: “I wish there was more combat in here. I wish the combat was better. I wish there was more opportunity to do stealth quests. I wish there was more dialogue with NPCs. But not once have I seen a review going: “Do you know what This RPG needs?

item.title: Arcanum Magick Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum magic design.

But first let me address what’s going on. New digs haven’t had time to even unpack the books into the bookshelves when Anubis1101 asked some really good questions And, as a super mutant, he gets a priority video, which means I just dropped what I was doing. I said “Stop Hammer time”, well, stop Arcanum time And did this video And it’ll be up. Um, I think Monday’s is already up, so it’ll be up on Wednesday. But then expect things to go back to the old digs for a while, because I had a lot of video in the queue. But anyway, let’s get into Anubis’s questions. He asked: “Tim, I’m really curious about the magic colleges in Arcanum and how you would approach such a system for a new game.

item.title: RPG Features Nobody Asked For
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about RPG features nobody asked for This is kind of a video for my fellow RPG game developers, because I keep seeing these features in RPGs and I don’t think anybody wants them and I don’t know what’s going on.

And I’m wondering sometimes if there’s a junior designer out there who’s just never seen these features before, even though they been in a lot of RPGs already, or. There’s this designer that thinks “Yeah, I’ve seen this done a lot, but this time I’m going to get it right”? No, No, you’re not. These features keep going at RPGs. They don’t work, and either, I’m sure, either QA complains about it or, oh my god, it ships, and then these bad reviews come out and then everybody’s unhappy, The designer’s unhappy, the company’s unhappy.

item.title: Respec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Funky Town, directions 2 and subloations within.

But oh no, today I’m talking about respec because I’m answering a question from protect one cloud, who asked: “Do you have a video on player respec, resetting talents, stats, character, investments? If not, might make a good video to go over”. Protect one cloud, You’re right, I haven’t made a video on this. I’ve mentioned it here and there in different videos, usually as an example of a feature that some people love, some people hate, or is there for balance reasons or meeting a design goal or whatever. But let me let’s talk about respec specifically and that way I can talk about all that in one spot and then point people to the one video. I think before I start, I should probably define respspec, because it means slightly different things to a lot of people. I’m talking about respec. My definition for respec is when a game lets you get back all of your skill perk attribute spell. Points, any combination of those, to respend them. Uh, some games let you change class too. I think that is a little rare and a little more extreme For purposes of this video. Think of respec as you’re allowed to rele the character that you’ve made, as if they were gaining all those levels again for the first time. Now, historically, respect didn’t used to exist.

item.title: Single vs. Multiplayer RPG Designs
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about getting jiggy with it or, more specifically, the application of jiggy oriented procedures to a variety of activities In particular.

Wait, actually today I’m answering a question about single versus multiplayer RPG designs. This question comes from Games by Mark Wolf, who asked: “Here’s a question for you, Tim: What are the pros and cons of solo RPGs versus multiplayer RPGs? Is there a difference? Further discussion point. My answer to the second question is that I would argue a solo RPG, if it amounts to nothing else, is an incomplete design.

item.title: Attributes Limiting Skills
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about attributes, limiting skills or, more specifically, what a.

A commenter asked a question about contradictions between attributes and skills. So let me let me give the question first and then I’ll dive into my response. The question was from Deja Vu6196, who asked: “Hi Tim, what are your thoughts on contradictions between attributes and skills? How can a player with a one intelligence reach 100 in the science skill? Should they be allowed to, or should the attribute cap the max skill they can reach”. Taking a specific example from Fallout New Vegas, the same one character who has a dumb dialogue can somehow reach speech 100 and logically talk legate Lanias into retreating, which also seems contradictory.

item.title: Topic vs. Branched Based Dialogs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about topic versus branchb-based dialogue in RPGs.

This is a question that came from Simon Dah 3196, who asked: “What are the key differences between a topic-based dialogue system and a branching kind of system, especially if the goal is depth, with game state conditions and speech attributes or something along those lines — lines? This is a good question, Not only because it’s a good question, It’s something I’ I’ve never talked about, but also I’ve never even thought about talking about this before. And I should start by saying I’ve only done a topicbased dialogue system one time, and that was in Fallout, and it was hybridized with a branching system. So if you played the first Fallout, you know that there was a branch. When you clicked on NBC up popped a dialogue, a branching dialogue. But over to the side you was tell me about or ask about, there was a little box and you could type in whatever topic you wanted. So it was kind of topic based, but we didn’t display the topics to You, as you learn things in the game, you could ask NPCs about it. You could ask them about Brahmen, you could ask them about mutants, you could ask them about ghouls. So it was one of these things that as the player went along, they could just bring up different topics to NPCs to see if any one of them said something new or different, and it led to anything. And this was our way of trying to kind of hide everything to the from the player and not obviously displaying it. But you had to guess a keyword to try to open up something.

item.title: The Premade Character
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pre-made character.

This comes from a question from Wes 9353, who asked: “Hey, Uncle Tim, do you have a video on the preset character? What determines the stats of the preset character and who determines the preset character”. I call these pre-made characters. Um, it’s just the term I use. I tried to do this with saying preset and I kept saying pre-made. So I finally decided I’m just going to call this the pre-made character and say why I’m not calling it preset. I don’t do this a lot, if you’ve played a lot of my RPGs, you’ll find that most of them do not have pre-made characters. I encourage people to make their own.

item.title: RPG Myths
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about RPG myths, and what do I mean by that?

I’m talking about lies that RPGs try to sell you, in other words, things that many — and I would even argue most — role playing games have in them, either as mechanics or just the way the story unfolds. That is not true in real life, just absolute fabrication.

item.title: Pros And Cons Of Long RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pros and cons of long RPGs.

This comes from a very interesting question that I got on my game length video a couple months ago, asked by Aleandro Gambino, who asked: “Assuming that the vast majority of CRPGs are long games, in your classification some RPGs are longer than others. What are the pros and cons of a 100 plus hour RPG compared to a 30 to 40hour one in your experience? That’s a good question because they’re absolutely right. There are RPGs tend to be longer than a lot of other games, especially one full session, you know, compared to like a rogike or card games or whatever that or shoot them ups, One session can be very short in those. So I do want to talk about this, and whenever I say long in the in this video, I will mean a 100 hours or more. And anything shorter than that isn’t a long RPG, if you need a concrete example to think of. Let’s compare Arcanum and Fallout. Fallout was designed to be about 20 to 40 hours to play through and the story was three acts: get the water chip, uh.

item.title: First vs. Third Person
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about first versus third person.

And by that I mean the viewpoint you get in the games you play. And of course, I’m going to talk about role playing games here, because that’s my thing. I get asked this question a lot and I’ve answered it a lot, but always in comments. But most recently I got asked by RoboCg, who asked: “What are the pros and cons of making RPG games as isometric versus firstperson, and what features are working better or worth worse in both scenarios in your opinion”. Similarly, to turn-based combats being better in isometric.

item.title: The Ultimate RPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the ultimate RPG.

Now, this is a fun Friday video, so what I want you to take from that is: I don’t know how to make this RPG, but I can certainly point you in the direction of this RPG. Hopefully one of you out there will find It’s a very simple question, I think, to answer, but way more difficult to execute. Basically, how can you make the RPG? It’s simple: make the nonviolence options in that RPG just as rich and fun as the violent ones and I think you have the ultimate RPG.

item.title: Deckbuilder RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about deck builder RPGs.

Those are role playing games where your abilities, or sometimes even more than that, are given to you randomly from a shuffle deck. This is not my area of expertise, but wami Bonito 2558 asked: would you have any opinions on carddeck Builder based art pgs, where character abilities are represented by cards and are also not guaranteed to play out the same way every combat, since in some fights you might never draw a particular card? Yes, I will tell you something. My response — you guessed it — is nuanced. I originally wrote a short response to this comment, but it wasn’t expressing the way I felt and I Al also thought that in writing it looked negative, which I’ve been told. Sometimes emails, my Memoir, comes across as negative and then when I say it, people are like: oh, I like it’s not negative at all. So I figured I’d do that with my answer to this. So, deck builder RPGs. Let me tell you why I like them. I haven’t played that many, but I will tell you why I like them and also why I like the — the whole idea behind that mechanic.

item.title: The Million XP Dungeon
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the million experience Point dungeon, or, based on how I’m dressed, this could also be called The Old Man and the C++- C++.

Anyway, the million XP dungeon is something I heard about over three decades ago. I think I heard about it in 1993. I was either at Gateway or orcon up in Los Angeles. Those were gaming conventions.

item.title: Violence As The Default In AAA RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about violence as the as the default in AAA RPGs.

It’s a very specific title because I mean all of this. I’m talking about violence. I’m talking about games that have violence as the default thing. That’s what they expect you to do, and I’m only talking about AAA RPGs because this came from a very good, thoughtful and well-written question by saber vs7, who asked: hi, Tim, I’m curious as to if you think games, while practically speaking, the RPG genre, specifically at the mainstream budget level — I know there’s plenty of Indies who are trying to be the change you want to see in this regard — will be able to evolve past the Paradigm of violence being the default way in which the player interacts with the world. Just so you know. This went on for a long time.

item.title: Solo vs. Party
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Solo versus party character control.

This is something that was asked most recently. I think it’s been asked several times, but it asked most recently by Tom FS, who asked: I was wondering if you could go over your thoughts on full party control versus only controlling your character. I view this separately from the full party creation versus individual character creation debate. What’s the lack of party control in Arcanum due to multiplayer? I know it was that case for never winner nights.

item.title: Worst RPG Ever
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the worst RPG ever.

I’m not going to talk about a specific RPG. I’m going to talk about what I would consider to be, and I will describe to you to be, the worst RPG you could possibly make. It’s a fun Friday. What I decided to do was I now have 400 videos. I’ve got hundreds of videos about what I consider to be the best way to make an RPG. Now, I don’t consider every decision I make in an RPG to be the best one or the best one for everyone. It’s just how I like to make an RPG. So I thought I’d flip this on the on its head and describe to you what an RPG would be like if you do the opposite of what I recommend on all those videos.

item.title: Perk Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about perk ideas.

This is a fun Friday video, so it’s kind of a me talking about ideas I’ve had in my idea book for years and I pulled out ones that were specific to perks. I wanted to do — and I’m going to talk about — reactions. I got to these perk ideas when I talked about them in the past and also why you don’t see these in any of my games. Get to all that, but first let’s just dive into some of the ideas I had for new perks. One of the ones I’ve always been noodling around is using your charisma attribute to replace your deck attribute for Armor class bonuses. Basically, whatever dexterity is providing to make you harder to hit, you can take a perk and say that bonus comes from my Charisma instead of my dexterity.

item.title: Pros And Cons Of GURPS As A CRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pros and cons of gerps as a computer role playing game, specifically what I learned trying to implement gerps as a computer game for Fallout.

This is to answer a question from player one fanatic who asks about, quote, giving a deep dive on gerps that was originally in Fallout. What skills, advantages, disadvantages and other game mechanics were implemented?

item.title: IP Idea: Unknown Powers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about an IP idea that I had for unknown Powers.

This is Friday, funay. I thought I’d throw out another idea that’s in my idea book and it’s something that’s bugged me for a while — for years actually — that many games that are in the magic or superhero or Supernatural game genre they just explain powers in like mundane, excruciating detail, as if your character was born with a manual and just goes: oh, I can fly 60 feet a second or something. And that takes away a lot of the — at least for me, a lot of the Mystique and weirdness and non scientificness of what’s going on. And I understand why tabletop games have to do this, but computer games don’t. The computer can understand exactly what you have without telling you. So that’s what I want to talk about. What if you made a game in one of those genres where you have a random power assigned to you when you make a? New game but you can’t use it right away, you it turns on at a random after a random time interval, or an event happens to you and I’ll explain all that, and then it only works under certain conditions.

item.title: Conflating Player Skill With Character Skill
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about conflating player skill with player character skill.

Now I’m answering a question by Vol forac, who asks how to resolve the conflict between player skill and their character’s ability in an action RPG. To elaborate with an example: Fallout in the past was all about skill checks and dice rolls, but starting with Fallout 3, the first person shooter aspect became more emphasized with each release. Where’s the line between letting the player be a good shot because the game mechanics allow them and the player being a good shot because they built a character? That is a good shot. Um, first of all, that’s a good question.

item.title: Ideas For A Generic Fantasy RPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about some ideas for a generic fantasy RPG.

Now I’ve already done some videos where I’ve talked my way through some notes I had on very specific IPS. These are IPS with you know, like I’ve always talked about, with defined settings and then a story, and then I’ve hinted at the mechanics I want to do.

But I found a document deep, in my archives. This one, this, the source of this document is troa in the early 2000s.

item.title: Troika's Conan The Barbarian Proposal
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about another game proposal I made.

This is back in June of 2004. I’ll talk about why I made it and what it was, but it was for. It was for the Conan, the Barbarian RPG. Um, I was doing some going through my files for reasons, and I found all kinds of interesting things, um, but one of the things I assembled across were was a cache of a bunch of proposals that we had written when vampire, the uh Masquerade bloodlines was winding down. I’ve talked about one of them, the B’s Gate 3 proposal, but this was one I totally forgot about This was entirely written by me. I will read you some of it, but let me give you a little backstory.

item.title: This Is How You Roleplay
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a channel I discovered that really showed me

this is how to role playay. I figured it’s Friday, let’s have some fun. And I’m going to talk about this YouTube channel. I found it’s called games on hard mode.

Maybe you already know about it. I’m going to link everything below. Um, I basically discovered this yesterday. I watched one and then I watched several more and then I grabbed a link and I sent it to Leonard Bari and said: you have to watch this guy play games and I’m going to show you some clips. I’m going to talk about why I love it, each, clip.

item.title: Replaying Diablo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about replaying Diablo.

I’m not talking about Diablo 4 or three or two. I’ve been playing the original Diablo. Why have I been playing the original Diablo? I will tell you at the end of this video, not because I want any sense of suspense, but I think it won’t make sense until I tell you all the things that I noticed playing Diablo. So let’s get into that, first of all, this game holds up really well for coming out in 1996 and being, I think it’s 640 480. I wonder if it’s 32200.

item.title: What Is An RPG?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what is an RPG?

Yes, I’m going to wade into this Minefield of trying to Define what a role playing game is, at least on a computer. I see people talk about this all the time. People look at some games — go: that’s not an RPG. And look at other games — go: that’s the quintessential RPG. And my whole career, and even before my career, I always viewed RPGs as a Continuum that there are some games that are more RPG like and others that are less. Especially lately, when games are coming out that people say have RPG elements, I’m like, okay, they never really Define RPG elements, but they talk about it. So what I want to do is, now that I’ve said, okay, I think RPG is a term that defines a Continuum of games, I want to talk about what that Continuum is, and you can think of this as a checklist of game features and the more of these features you have. The more I would consider you an RPG, and then I’ll talk about how other people view this when I’m done.

item.title: Bored Now...
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. I get asked to review games or comment on other games a lot and I’ve said I’m not going to do that. But today I thought I’d do something a little silly, and I mean this light-heartedly. But I’m going to talk about times I’ve been playing games, other RPGs — where I get bored. Bo I’m going to talk about — I’m not going to about particular games.

item.title: Bugs In RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about bugs and particularly bugs and RPGs.

My experience is all in RPGs so I’m sure a lot of what I’m saying will generalize, but I just want to talk about why RPGs tend to be buggy, and this actually comes from a question a lot of people had questions. This is when I picked rrams 1989 asked: will all RPG games always be very buggy on release?

item.title: Morality Systems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about morality systems in RPGs.

This is actually a question from the discording tales 8198, who asks: what is your point of view on alignment or Karma systems in RPGs, systems of morality, of moralities, maybe, IE going Beyond good or evil, but not relativistic per se, using different sets of values measured from their own viewpoints? Hm, that is a bit of a vague question, um, but it’s also a difficult one, because now we’re talking, philosophy and definition of Good and Evil, and let me talk about what I tried in a couple games and then what I’m thinking now. So back in Fallout, we tried to do reputation and we just did kind of an overall reputation. So it wasn’t necessarily good or evil, it was. This was a positive thing to do towards your rep and this was a negative thing, and it was done via script and it turned out to be really varied, because what one group of people in on the team thought was a good thing for you to do for your reputation, other people thought was a bad thing. And in the game itself there was: hey, if you do this would be positive only for this one group. It’s a negative for this other group. So I don’t think Fallout was a good first step. It was a, mish mash of ideas. I mean, we had reputation titles in there too, like a champion of some group or you know, the child killer was a reputation title. So when we did Arcanum, I wanted to systematize these things.

item.title: Making Good Factions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about making good factions for your games.

This is something that every time I go into making a setting and remember — I think setting is the first step of what you need to do to make a game — I always try to think of what kind of factions are going to be there. And just to be clear what I’m going to talk about, I’m talking about designing the factions, not writing dialogues for particular characters. I’m not good at that — but I am talking about making factions that you’ve designed so that they seem like they belong in your world and have their own goals and are just fun to interact with. I’m going to talk about how I think you should go about doing that.

item.title: Gameplay's Influence On Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game plays: influence on story.

Moji XOXO asks: how much does gameplay influence what stories are or can be told in video games? How do developers grapple with the tension of a story that wants to do one thing or go a certain way, while ensuring that gameplay stays consistently fun and engaging — engaging, throughout? That is a good question. I think I’ve touched on this in other videos a bit, but I’ll just restate it here. It is very, very challenging for video game narrative designers to stop thinking linearly. Most of them are used to writing stories, whether they’re for books or movies or TV shows, and those proceed linearly where games — at least RPG games, which is what I’m talking about here — do not, or mine don’t, and that’s something I want to talk about. So the first red flag to me goes off when I’m having meetings with narrative designers and they say things like: the player goes here: and the player does this and the first thing I say is what if the player goes there and does something else?

item.title: Top Five Modern RPG Masterclass
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about my top five modern RPG master class before I start.

New camera setup. It’s mounted, it’s a little higher, it’s a little farther back.

Let’s see how this goes. I’m playing around with things, so what do I mean by the top five modern RPG Master Class? Well, I did video about a month ago of my top five video games, and these are the games that I grew up with and the ones that I thought were the most influential in how I made games in my career. That’s not what these five RPGs are. These are going to be five role playing games that I think are the best to learn from, as certain aspects of them for game development and specifically for RPG development do I think these are the best games ever.

item.title: Skill Design in RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about skill design in RPGs in particular.

Queen of bad jokes 9413 asks: I wondered how you would approach skills for a new game. How do you choose? What skill types are there? How do you and would you level them? Is there a particular skill that has worked out for multiple games? How can you design for balancing? And are there skill tables too? I mean testing besides in the testing rooms and player testing in the alpha Queen.

item.title: A Christmas Lesson In CRPG Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim.

I hope you’re all having a good holiday. I’m secular but I do enjoy a good Christmas and I think if I could pull off a good Papa Hemingway, I think I can also pull off a halfway decent Santa Claus. Anyway, I thought that because today is Christmas, I would read an essay I wrote in 2002 at tra, and this was why we were making Temple of Elemental Evil. I wrote it during the holiday period. There it’s called a Christmas lesson in crpg design, so let me just read this to you now. I wrote this, like I said, 21 years ago. So there we go every year at tricky games. We have a gift change at Christmas time, or Hanukkah or Quanza, depending on who you ask.

item.title: Loot Tables
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about loot tables, or my theory of loot tables, and I’ll talk about what a loot table is, and then I’m going to talk about my theory and the literally hardcore design elements that go into deciding what items go on to loot tables.

Um, I’m going to bring up a screenshot examples of what I mean. This is kind of intended to be an extension of what I talked about in the game economy video, so you may want to watch that. This is: uh, I realized when I was talking about how I decide what items in up on what loot tables. I described it at a very high level. I kind of want to go into that in more detail.

item.title: The Evolution Of Flaws
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the evolution of flaws.

If you played The Outer Worlds, you know in there flaws is a system mechanic where as you play the game, it pays attention to things you do and then suggest, suggests a flaw you know, like maybe you’re scared of robots, or maybe you take a lot of falling damage — a lot, or you’re, maybe you’re afraid of heights because you take a lot of falling damage. The idea for that germinated almost 37 years before that game. Let me walk you through this, and I think this is a good parallel to the video I did on why ideas aren’t worthwhile — because this idea, I mean, came to me when I was a teenager but it took decades for it to be honed into something that I put into an RPG the way I wanted it to be. So let’s talk about that, so I know I’ve told these stories. I’m going to kind of repeat them here a little bit. Just for completeness, but back in high school when I was playing D and D, there was a couple times where low character stats led to really interesting characters. One of them was dumb dialogue which actually ended up in Fallout.

item.title: Tabletop RPG Influences
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about tabletop RPG influences that directly show up in my own computer games.

Specifically, I want to talk about experiences I had either as a DM or as a player that resulted in something going in my game. In fact, I’m going to answer the question from mud MW 4321, who asks: hey, Tim, I remember you talking about your super luck character from gerps, which made you want to put luck into Fallout. Is there anything else in your crpgs that was heavily influenced by your experience in tabletop games? This also goes for narratives that got told at the table and then put into your game.

item.title: Lord Of Light CRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer another question, this one from aramel whisperwind, who asks.

I read Lord of Light a couple days ago, based on your recommendation in one of your earlier videos. Now I’m sitting here thinking about your notes and how it would translate into a crpg. Perhaps you could talk about that.

Okay, my favorite book — this is my Eastman press copy, which is gilded and leather bound, smells really good. So I looked through that and looked through some of my notes and this is what I came up with. So, first of all, I’ve always talked about the three important things about any RPG that you design first, which is setting, mechanics and story. So let’s talk about what I would do for those three things for Lord of Light. First of all, setting. I think this setting very much needs to support exploration by the player. That’s what you want to do in an RPG. You want to get out there, so what I would? Do is I’d go through the book and look, at all the really cool places that are mentioned and sometimes only lightly touched upon but never really fleshed out by zelazny. For example, he mentions a bunch of cities, but he barely talks about what’s in them.

item.title: Class-based vs. Skill-based RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about class versus skill based role-playing games.

I’ve read a lot of discussion online about this for years. Some people think classes are the way to go, their original, their how things were done a long time ago. They’re a tried and true and tested. I’ve heard other people say, no, skills are the way to go, they’re the best, they’re the new wave, even though they’ve been around for 20 years. I think that the real answer, of course, is: uh, a little one, little the other. So what I wanted to do is talk about the pros and cons of class-based systems, because I realized every Pro of class is a con of skill and vice versa. So I’ll talk about.

item.title: Elden Ring and EverQuest
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Elden ring and EverQuest, and you may be going.

What do these games have to do with each other? I will explain, so I know I said I was going to review a game. Please don’t consider this a review of either Elder ring or EverQuest. I was talking with a friend of mine a few months ago and I mentioned that Elden ring was one of my favorite games from last year, from 2022, and he was really surprised because, as he reminded me, I wasn’t really into the Dark Souls games. I didn’t hate them, they just weren’t for me. I found them to be very linear and I found the combat to be very choreographed. It’s the same reason I liked most of wow, but I didn’t like Wow’s raids because those attacks felt like dance choreography. It was like attack.

item.title: What I Like In An RPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what I like in an RPG, and what I mean by that is: what elements do I like to see in RPGs that I play, which are, unsurprisingly, elements and RPGs that I made?

I don’t want to get into the whole what — what is an RPG versus what’s an action game versus what’s an adventure game — but I will say that the more of these elements I see in an RPG, the more likely I am to like it and enjoy it, and the fewer of these I see, well then I lean towards not liking it. And if there’s too few, I do kind of question whether you made an RPG. These are things that I think I’ve had for a long time. I remember having these discussions at interplay in the 90s: an Enterprise RPGs and some of them I question like: are you sure you should be doing that? That does not feel RPGs to me, so I try to walk these here. I try to put them into different categories and then talk about in each category.

item.title: Troika's Baldur's Gate 3 Proposal
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troika’s botters Gate 3 proposal.

I found this in a folder I didn’t expect to see it in. I was looking up something about Temple of Elemental Evil to see if I could find the date of the initial proposal that we wrote, and I found Troika bg3 proposal dated July 2003.. So this would have been made before I even finished Temple, because that was finished in the fall of 2003, and before I went on to vampire. So I read through it and I thought I’d give you an idea of what we were planning to make.

item.title: Attribute Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about attributes in role-playing games in general, because the last couple days

I’ve talked about specifically the attributes in Fallout and in Arcanum and made me think, wow, you know, not only have my thoughts changed since then there. I’ve been in so many discussions about attributes, I’ve seen lots of RPGs that handle our attributes differently. Plus, someone even mentioned in the comments that I was saying how Dex was so overpowered in gerps in a much earlier video where I talked about the gerps apps I made, including a combat one, a combat simulator. But then in Fallout and in Arcanum I did the same thing. And you’re right, I’m totally guilty. I’m.
